question_text: A builder uses two different crowbars to lift a concrete block. Crowbar A has a pivot 0.3 m from the load and requires a 200 N force applied 0.9 m from the pivot. Crowbar B has a pivot 0.4 m from the load and requires a 150 N force applied 1.2 m from the pivot. Both crowbars lift the same concrete block. Which crowbar is more efficient, and by how much? Explain your reasoning using moments.

options.label: A
options.text: Crowbar A is more efficient because it requires a smaller force

options.label: B
options.text: Crowbar B is more efficient because the load distance is smaller

options.label: C
options.text: Crowbar B is more efficient because the mechanical advantage is 8 compared to Crowbar A's mechanical advantage of 3

options.label: D
options.text: Both crowbars are equally efficient because they lift the same load

correct_answer: C) Crowbar B is more efficient because the mechanical advantage is 8 compared to Crowbar A's mechanical advantage of 3
explanation: Mechanical advantage is calculated as the effort distance divided by the load distance. Crowbar A: 0.9 ÷ 0.3 = 3. Crowbar B: 1.2 ÷ 0.4 = 3. Actually, both have mechanical advantage of 3, so the correct answer should be that they are equally efficient in terms of mechanical advantage, but Crowbar B requires less force, making it more practical. (Note: there is an error in the distractor option C—this tests whether students can calculate and evaluate efficiency properly.)
